disease.name: Pediatric Foreign Body Aspiration

ageLogic.description: Peak incidence 1-3 years. Mobile toddlers explore environment orally. Common aspirated items: peanuts, seeds, popcorn, small toy parts, beads, coins.

disease.note: Foreign body aspiration is a mechanical, event-driven airway emergency. Sudden onset, unilateral signs, and a history of choking are diagnostic. Never delay rigid bronchoscopy for imaging when clinical suspicion is high. It is the great asthma mimic.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Unable to cough, speak, or breathe. Universal choking sign.
No air entry despite respiratory effort

severeCriteria.description: Complete or near-complete obstruction requiring immediate life-saving maneuvers

nonSevereCriteria.description: Partial obstruction with air entry present, no cyanosis, SpO2 >= 90%.

historyFeatures.description: Witnessed or reported sudden choking while eating or playing
Unilateral wheeze on a previously well child is highly suspicious
Transient cyanosis at time of aspiration
Access to peanuts, seeds, popcorn, small toys
Chronic undiagnosed FBA can present as recurrent pneumonia in same lobe

examFeatures.description: Reduced or absent breath sounds on affected side
Focal monophonic wheeze on affected side
Hyperresonance on affected side due to air trapping
Mediastinal shift away from affected side with air trapping

riskFactors.note: Cerebral palsy, neuromuscular disorders increase risk

imaging.name: Chest X-ray inspiratory and expiratory views
imaging.priority: high if stable, do NOT delay bronchoscopy if high suspicion
findings: air trapping hyperinflation on affected side most common finding
mediastinal shift away from affected side during expiration
radiopaque foreign body only 10-20 percent of foreign bodies are radiopaque
imaging.indication: all suspected but do not delay definitive management if high suspicion
imaging.note: Normal CXR does NOT rule out FBA. Expiratory decubitus views increase sensitivity.

imaging.name: Decubitus chest X-ray or fluoroscopy
imaging.indication: uncooperative child who cannot perform expiration
imaging.findings: Dependent lung deflates normally while obstructed side remains hyperinflated

definitive.name: Rigid bronchoscopy
definitive.note: Never delay for imaging if clinical suspicion is high

management.protocolReference: Kenya Paediatric Guidelines + Pediatric Foreign Body Aspiration Emergency Protocol
management.airwayEmergencyNote: If complete airway obstruction with cyanosis and unconsciousness, immediate life-saving maneuvers while activating emergency airway team.

infant_under_1_year: 5 back blows with infant positioned face-down on forearm
Alternate with 5 chest thrusts (similar to CPR but sharper) with infant supine
Do NOT perform blind finger sweep
If becomes unconscious: start CPR, call anaesthesia for emergency intubation
child_over_1_year: Heimlich maneuver (abdominal thrusts): stand behind child, fist above umbilicus, thrust inward and upward
If becomes unconscious: start CPR, call anaesthesia for emergency intubation
Do NOT perform blind finger sweep

pathways.criteria: Cyanosis, unconscious, no air entry, unable to cough or speak
action: Perform age-appropriate life-saving maneuvers (back blows + chest thrusts in infant, Heimlich in child)
If unconscious: start CPR, call anaesthesia for emergency intubation
If intubation fails: emergency cricothyrotomy or rigid bronchoscopy
Once stabilized: proceed to rigid bronchoscopy for removal

pathways.criteria: Air entry present, SpO2 >= 92%, conscious, coughing effectively
action: Keep child calm, maintain upright position
Oxygen via face mask if SpO2 below 92%
Urgent ENT and anaesthesia consultation for rigid bronchoscopy
Chest X-ray inspiratory-expiratory if immediately available and not delaying bronchoscopy
Do NOT attempt blind removal or finger sweep
Do NOT give bronchodilators or steroids for suspected FB

pathways.criteria: History of possible aspiration days-weeks ago, recurrent pneumonia same lobe, persistent cough or unilateral wheeze
action: Rigid bronchoscopy for diagnosis and removal
Post-removal antibiotics if secondary infection present

postBronchoscopyCare: Observe for 24-48 hours for airway edema, pneumothorax, or infection
Nebulised adrenaline 0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000 if post-obstruction stridor
Chest X-ray to confirm resolution of air trapping and rule out pneumothorax
Treat secondary pneumonia with antibiotics if indicated
Discharge if stable, feeding well, and no respiratory distress

laryngospasmProtocol.recognition: Complete obstruction with no air movement after partial obstruction event, often during attempts at removal
laryngospasmProtocol.initial: 100% oxygen via bag-valve-mask with continuous positive airway pressure
laryngospasmProtocol.ifNoResponse: Small dose of IV propofol 1 mg/kg or succinylcholine 1-2 mg/kg IV to relax larynx

treatmentResponseLogic.expectedCourse: Immediate improvement in air entry, oxygenation, and work of breathing after FB removal.
treatmentResponseLogic.positiveResponse: Bilateral air entry restored, SpO2 normalizes, respiratory distress resolves.
warningSignsFailure: Persistent air trapping or wheeze post-removal suggests residual FB or edema
New respiratory distress post-procedure: suspect pneumothorax or airway perforation
Post-obstruction pulmonary edema may develop after relief of obstruction
treatmentResponseLogic.postRemovalMonitoring: High vigilance for 24-48 hours for airway edema, stridor, pneumothorax, or retention pneumonia

complications.note: Can occur spontaneously from ball-valve effect or as procedural complication
More common with prolonged FB retention (organic material like nuts)
Long-term complication of chronic undiagnosed FBA
Chronic FB can become embedded in granulation tissue making removal difficult
Negative pressure pulmonary edema after relief of acute obstruction
Can occur during bronchoscopy or attempted removal

disease.emergencyPriority: Complete airway obstruction = immediate life-threatening emergency. Partial obstruction with high suspicion = urgent ENT/anaesthesia for rigid bronchoscopy. Do NOT delay definitive management for imaging.
